content.problemStatement:
Suppose that you have several powers of two, specifically, you have AiA_iAi​ numbers equal to 2i2^i2i for each 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N. You want to divide **all** of these numbers into two groups with equal sum. However, it's not always possible.

To make it possible, in one operation you are allowed to choose any 1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N and receive an extra number 2i2^i2i (in other words, you can increase any AiA_iAi​ by 111). Let's define f(A1,A2,…,AN)f(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N)f(A1​,A2​,…,AN​) as the smallest number of operations that you need to make to be able to split the numbers into two groups with equal sums.

You are given NNN integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​. Find the sum of f(A1,A2,…,AN)f(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N)f(A1​,A2​,…,AN​) over all tuples (A1,A2,…,AN)(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N)(A1​,A2​,…,AN​) with 0≤Ai≤Bi0 \le A_i \le B_i0≤Ai​≤Bi​ (there are (B1+1)(B2+1)…(BN+1)(B_1+1)(B_2+1)\ldots (B_N+1)(B1​+1)(B2​+1)…(BN​+1) of them). As this number may be very large, output it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN −-− the number of integers.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output the answer modulo 998244353998244353998244353 on a new line.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases doesn't exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There are 222 tuples: (0,0,0)(0, 0, 0)(0,0,0) and (0,1,0)(0, 1, 0)(0,1,0). We have f(0,0,0)=0f(0, 0, 0) = 0f(0,0,0)=0 (there are no numbers, so divide them into two empty sets), and f(0,1,0)=1f(0, 1, 0) = 1f(0,1,0)=1 (we have one number, namely 21=22^1 = 221=2. Use one operation to receive a second copy of 212^121 and then place these copies in different sets). The answer is thus 0+1=10 + 1 = 10+1=1
Test case 222: There are 666 tuples, with their function values as follows:

f(0,0,0)=0f(0, 0, 0) = 0f(0,0,0)=0
f(0,0,1)=1f(0, 0, 1) = 1f(0,0,1)=1 (receive 222^222)
f(1,0,0)=1f(1, 0, 0) = 1f(1,0,0)=1 (receive 202^020)
f(1,0,1)=2f(1, 0, 1) = 2f(1,0,1)=2 (receive 202^020 and 212^121, then split as {20,20,21}\{2^0, 2^0, 2^1\}{20,20,21} and {22}\{2^2\}{22}, both with sum 444)
f(2,0,0)=0f(2, 0, 0) = 0f(2,0,0)=0
f(2,0,1)=1f(2, 0, 1) = 1f(2,0,1)=1 (receive 222^222)

Add all these up to get 555, which is the output.
